title: Quiz: Pulse Shaping for High-Fidelity Gates
description: Test your understanding of pulse shapes, spectral analysis, and their impact on gate fidelity

questions.question: Why are square pulses problematic for high-fidelity quantum gates?

options.text: They are too slow to implement fast gates
Their spectrum has slowly decaying sidelobes (1/f decay) that can excite unwanted transitions
They cannot produce arbitrary rotation angles
They require more power than other pulse shapes

questions.explanation: Square pulses have a sinc-function spectrum with sidelobes that decay only as 1/f. This slow decay means significant spectral power exists at frequencies far from the carrier, potentially exciting the |1⟩→|2⟩ transition and causing leakage errors.

questions.question: What is special about Gaussian pulses in the frequency domain?

options.text: They have zero bandwidth
Their Fourier transform is also a Gaussian, with no sidelobes
They produce a flat spectrum
They concentrate all power at the carrier frequency

questions.explanation: The Fourier transform of a Gaussian is also a Gaussian. This means the spectrum has no oscillatory sidelobes - it decays exponentially rather than polynomially. This makes Gaussian pulses ideal for minimizing spectral leakage.

questions.question: For a transmon with anharmonicity α = -250 MHz, what determines the amount of leakage to the |2⟩ state during a gate?

options.text: The pulse duration only
The spectral amplitude of the pulse at frequency ω₀₁ + α (the 1-2 transition)
The phase of the microwave drive
The temperature of the dilution refrigerator

questions.explanation: Leakage to the |2⟩ state is driven by spectral components of the pulse at the 1-2 transition frequency, which is ω₁₂ = ω₀₁ + α. The leakage probability is proportional to |Ω̃(ω₁₂)|², the squared spectral amplitude at this frequency.

questions.question: What is a Gaussian square (flat-top Gaussian) pulse commonly used for?

options.text: Only for Z rotations
Readout pulses and two-qubit cross-resonance gates where sustained drive is needed
Reducing the total gate time
Eliminating the need for calibration

questions.explanation: Gaussian square pulses combine smooth Gaussian edges (to minimize spectral leakage) with a flat-top section (to provide sustained drive). They are ideal for qubit readout and cross-resonance gates where longer drive times are needed without excessive spectral spreading.

questions.question: What is the time-bandwidth product, and what does it tell us?

options.text: The product of gate time and qubit frequency; it determines the rotation angle
A measure of pulse efficiency; lower values mean the pulse achieves the same spectral confinement in less time
The ratio of coherence time to gate time; it determines maximum number of operations
The product of amplitude and duration; it equals the rotation angle

questions.explanation: The time-bandwidth product Δt×Δf characterizes pulse efficiency. A lower value means the pulse achieves good spectral confinement (narrow bandwidth) in shorter time. Gaussian pulses achieve the theoretical minimum of ~0.44, while square pulses are ~0.89.

questions.question: When truncating a Gaussian pulse at ±nσ, what is the typical choice for n and why?

options.text: n = 1, to minimize gate time
n = 2-4, to balance pulse smoothness with practical duration
n = 10, to maximize spectral purity
n = 0.5, to maximize the Rabi frequency

questions.explanation: Typically n = 2-4 (pulse extends 2-4 standard deviations from center). Too small truncation creates discontinuities that add spectral components. Too large truncation makes the pulse unnecessarily long. 3σ truncation retains 99.7% of the Gaussian area.

questions.question: A Blackman window pulse has better sidelobe suppression than a Gaussian pulse, but at the cost of a wider main lobe (slower decay near the carrier frequency).

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! The Blackman window achieves excellent sidelobe suppression (first sidelobe at -58 dB) but has a wider main lobe than Gaussian. This is a general tradeoff: better sidelobe suppression typically requires a wider main lobe, which means slower spectral rolloff near the carrier.

questions.question: If a gate must be faster than the timescale 1/|α| set by the anharmonicity, what advanced techniques can help maintain fidelity?

options.text: Simply increase the pulse amplitude without any corrections
Use DRAG pulses or optimal control to actively cancel leakage
Use square pulses instead of shaped pulses
Decrease the qubit frequency to increase α

questions.explanation: When gates must be faster than 1/|α| (~4 ns for typical transmons), pulse shaping alone is insufficient. DRAG (Derivative Removal by Adiabatic Gate) pulses add a derivative component that cancels leakage. Optimal control techniques can further improve fidelity by optimizing the entire pulse shape.
